speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 4, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Kraus. My name is Jill Greenberg. We're in Brookline, Massachusetts, in the United States of America. And this interview is being conducted in English. Today is June 4, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Kraus. My name is Jill Greenberg. We're in Brookline, Massachusetts, in the United States of America. And this Interview is being conducted in English. Stand by. Stand by. And we're rolling. Could you please say and spell your current name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Kraus-- M-I-C-H-A-E-L K-R-A-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please say and spell your first and last name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michl Kraus, M-I-CH-L K-R-A-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you ever called by any other name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends and my parents always called me Misa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- in Czech, it was M-I-S with a little accent circumflex inverted and an A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on June 28, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age is?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in? And you could you please spell it-- and what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Trutnov, T-R-U-T-N-O-V, which was in Bohemia, which is now the Czech Republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Kraus, what do you remember of your childhood before the war? What was Jewish life like? What was family life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We belonged to the middle class of a small town that was nestled in a little valley overlooked by a beautiful castle. The town had about 15,000 inhabitants that was famous for its textiles that was exported all over the world. And many of the Jewish inhabitants were involved in textiles, either as owners or directors of mills. My father was a physician that had a large family practice that consisted of all walks of life. There were the wealthy people down to peasants that lived in the outlying villages. So we were relatively well-off, lived in a villa in a large garden. I only found out after the war that that villa didn't really belong to my father but to his brother. My childhood was obviously without any worries until the 1938 fall when Czechoslovakia lost its border regions to Hitler, the Sudentenland. We-- I did not have any brothers. I was the only child and was probably somewhat spoiled and had everything that I wanted. I went to school, to elementary school, which was almost on the same street from where we lived. And to the best of my knowledge, I was the only Jewish child in that particular school. Most of the other Jewish children went to another elementary school, which was closer to the center of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any discrimination that you were aware of as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was certainly-- I wouldn't call it at the beginning discrimination. But I was different. And I think children are very sensitive and often cruel about differences. And the difference was primarily manifested when we had religious instructions, which I think only happened once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school-- this was a state that had separation of religion and state so that during the religious instructions, the Catholics would go to one classroom and the Protestant the other. And I had a hour, a free hour, that I could go and run outside. The Jewish children had religious instructions. And we will learned Hebrew in an old what used to be the Jewish school in the Jewish street in the center of town, where an old cantor taught us how to read. And also, we had some instructions how to dress the Torah. That was for the boys. But other than that, I would visit the synagogue primarily on high holy days with my mother. And all the big holidays were also celebrated at my uncle's, who was the head of the Jewish community and had a home that observed all the Jewish dietary laws, such as you know, could not eat meat and, and milk products at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- what did it mean being the head of the Jewish community there? What kinds of responsibilities did he have? Or what role did he play?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you exactly, because I was a child then. But he probably was like the head of the synagogue here, although I think the organization was somewhat different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this town the same town that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was born in a clinic run by Dr. Ganz, who later immigrated to Palestine. And curiously enough, my wife's parents who knew him well because they were living in Palestine. And many of the, shall we call it, bourgeois families would, would have their children born in this clinic, which was about two hours dis-distance by car from where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about this clinic that the Jew-- why did the Jewish families choose to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it was exclusively Jewish. But it was just-- I, I suppose he was not only a good obstetrician, but the clinic was probably set up nicer than having a midwife come to the house or going to a public hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother generally helped my father when he was very busy with patients or preparing them, but then was-- the main occupation was simply being a mother and supervise the household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever remember hearing your parents talk about any of the politics that were going on or any concern?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it actually started for us, for me, in 1938 when Czechoslovakia mobilized in late summer because of the threat of war from Hitler's Germany. And we were evacuated. My mother and I were evacuated to the center of Bohemia, because our town was right on the German bor-- border with Germany. The, the frontier was maybe five kilometers or less from our house. And at-- all during 1937 and '38, Czechoslovakia built a very strong defense line-- this was called the Second Maginot Line-- on its borders to be able to defend itself. So when, when mobilization was declared, my father, who was a physician, stayed behind. And we went to a cousin of ours in a, in a little-- in a smaller town where I went to a school with more kind of country kids. And that's where I learned how to stand on my head, which I can still do today.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. Were you aware why this movement was going on, why you were suddenly being taken from your home and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so, even though I was seven and then eight years old. It-- you know, we were-- even there, we were digging air raid shelters. And it, it was a pretty grim situation. I was personally not aware that this was particularly dangerous for Jew-- for us Jews. But later on that year, there was-- here and there, there were kind of remarks dropped inadvertently, I suppose, or out of-- not necessarily out of spite-- by kids, you know, that I'm a dirty Jew. But that happened very seldom, at least at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember wonder anything-- your response to their saying that? Or were, were you ever told to conceal your identity as a Jew, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was never told to, to conceal it, although I, I, I think the only way that children would know that was because of that religious one hour of week when I was n-- with neither the Catholics nor the Protestants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the home like that you were in in the country with your uncle? When you and your mother left where you were living with your father, you said you moved out to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we moved into a beautiful villa of some very wealthy relatives where we have everything that we wanted. And we were even picked up by, by their limousine and their chauffeurs. So we didn't, we didn't seem to be at all deprived of anything. And there were no shortages at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did pe-- did you have a sense that people were worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because there were, there were people-- and the men were called in to the armed forces. And it was certainly a very tense time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, three or four months-- and then when the Munich conference took place and the so-called Allies gave part of Czechoslovakia to Hitler, that was the end of that crisis. That was a tremendous let down for the nation. And we went back home-- not for long, because six months later, Hitler marched into the rest of Czechoslovakia. And since we were on the border, we were one of the first to see the troops move in. And I recall waking up early one morning. It was still almost dark. And my parents were glued to what was a radio receiver, which was a very primitive kind of crystal set. And one could hardly hear what was going on. And they told me that I should go to school as usual and that the Germans are moving into town. And that same day, we learned that my father's sister, who was an elderly lady, lived right on the main square of this little town where the Germans stopped to rest the, the troops. And it was snowing lightly. It was very a kind of a cold, damp morning. And I heard that my aunt came down and gave the soldiers some hot tea or hot coffee. And a few hours later, she poisoned herself. She took her own life. So she was the first victim and also the first funeral that I attended and maybe the only one, because there were no funerals thereafter. And that was the first time that I went to the new cemetery that was open only a year or so and participated and at the services.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things gradually changed. Every few weeks, there would be a new proclamation, new laws, new regulations, that would restrict our lives. The restrictions would primarily apply to Jews, less so to the general population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what some of those restrictions were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jews were not allowed to have bicycles and fur coats and silverware and watches and gold. My father first could only treat-- well, first, his, his insurance company informed him that he can no longer treat patients for the state insurance company, which was probably at least 50% of his practice. He then was not allowed to treat non-Jews. And eventually, he had to completely close his office. Jews were not allowed to go out after eight o'clock. They were not allowed to go to some places of public meetings, [INAUDIBLE] parks, coffee houses, restaurants. There were only few designated places where they could eat or shop. And bit by bit, they were also restricted as far as how many square feet of living space they were allowed to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you specifically remember anything that you were particularly told as a child? Was your bicycle taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's a very good example. In about 1938, my birthday, I got my first red bicycle that adored and polished and was very proud of it. And it seemed to me that very early in the occupation, we had to turn our bicycles in. And that was a big personal blow to me. As far as-- well, I was most affected, of course, that I was not allowed to go to school. I only went to school for four years. So in 1940, I-- about a year of after the occupation started, I was-- I had to stay home. And Jewish families arranged private tutors to teach us in groups in our houses for the first year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you study both religious and non-religious subjects at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But I think that probably, they were mostly non-religious. And then it was not really well-organized instruction. It was kind of an ad hoc arrangement. The only thing I do remember is that I studied painting or drawing. But that stopped very soon, because the professor was not a Jew and that he was not allowed to give Jews lessons. And I think we were not allowed to travel between towns unless we had special permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about things like food? Did you still have food available to you? Or were there some kinds of shortages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, bit by bit, food was rationed. There were coupons issued. And it seems to me that Jews received smaller rations than Christians. Then at first, we had to take lodgers into our house. And we had to give up several rooms. And the first lodger was a family of our janitor who lived with us so that we would reduce our living space. And then a few months later, my uncle, who, who was, I found on later, the owner of the house, had to move out of his villa in another town and move into a room or two in our-- in where I lived. Then eventually, about six months or eight months before we were deported, we had to give the house to a German officer and move into the Jewish street into an old, decrepit building with other Jewish families. And there, we had one room that didn't have any running water. I recall that every day, I had to go to the main square where there was a fountain. And I was carrying two buckets of water. And in one point, I was attacked by a bunch of kids in German Hitlerjugend uniforms that roughed me up and called me all sorts of names. But otherwise, I was never really physically abused at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father's practice basically was ended during this time. How did your family survive financially?

speaker: interviewee
text: From savings-- but even those were blocked. So it was-- of course, the needs were rather limited. And it was primarily just food. But there were reprisals. At one event, the, the local-- at the local railway station, there was an explosion. And the gasoline petrol dump was blown up. And it was declared to be a sabotage. And many hostages were taken, mostly Jewish, who were then sent to Mauthausen. And every fifth was shot. And those-- the others were returned after several months. And they were really in very bad shape. That was our first introduction to concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of all of this as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about it. But I don't think it really meant all that much to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long were you on the street that your parents or, or your family was forced to move to where the Jews lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six or eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- did they have an ad hoc school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't believe that at that time, I did much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to go outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we had restricted hours and couldn't go everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if you play--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could only play with other Jewish children. And I meant, at, at first during the occupation, we would visit each other. But I think slowly, most Christians were afraid to meet Jews and did in a clandestine way. But there were many courageous people. And some of them also hid valuable things for my parents, such as cameras and watches and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hid them for or from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hid them for them. And this is how even the family album was saved and some of the valuable papers. Birth certificates and so on that I have today were with a bank clerk over-- during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they returned them to me voluntarily after the war, even though I had no clue that they had any of our objects.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point, was most of your family still alive, as far as you knew-- your aunts and your uncles and cousins and--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm sorry-- at this point while you were living on the Jewish street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody has-- of the immediate family-- has perished until then.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were on the-- you lived in the street for about six months. What happened afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was really the beginning of the deportation. On the 15th of December, 1942, we all had to assemble at the local railway station and were sent to the county seat, where we stayed in the high school for one or two nights. And Jews were taken there from the whole county and then shipped by train to Theres-- to the ghetto of Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many people were in the train or in the car of the train that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were kind of normal passenger trains. This was still fairly humane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were allowed to bring whatever possessions you wanted with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were limited to something like two large suitcases at that time. And the limit really was also that at that time, the train station to Theresienstadt stopped in a town about five or 10 kilometers from Theresienstadt. And everybody had to walk there and carry their luggage. So that was a little also a limiting factor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the feeling was? Were people scared? Were they panicked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, they were-- I know that there were several families who took their lives the night before the deportation. So there was definitely apprehension. Certainly was the no camping trip. And the fe-- you know, the fear of what are the most important things that we had to take stays with me till to today. Whenever I have to travel, I'm always extremely anxious of what I pack, as if I was never coming back or that wherever I'm going, you not buy anything or obtain anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you wanted to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.
